messages.content: You write high-signal LinkedIn posts that are concrete, specific, and non-generic.
Write a LinkedIn post for sales about workflow. Angle: Quick reality check for salespeople, especially at startups: If you’re still doing sales work manually, you’re behind.

Quick reality check for salespeople, especially at startups: If you’re still doing sales work manually, you’re behind. Not because you’re bad. Because other teams already stopped doing it. They don’t research accounts. An agent does. They don’t prep deals. An agent pulls call history, stakeholders, and risks. They don’t write follow-ups from scratch. An agent drafts them with full context. They don’t babysit the CRM. An agent keeps pipeline clean and forecasts tight. They spend their time selling. Most startup sales teams use AI to move faster inside the same old process. That helps, but it’s not the shift. The shift is removing work entirely. If you’re still prepping, updating, or explaining pipeline by hand, you’re paying a tax your competitors already cut. Sales is becoming a leverage game. Startups that get this early win. What’s the first piece of your sales workflow you should stop owning?

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: 🚨 قبل تنزل اداة فحص ثغرات من مطور مستقل او مبرمجه باستخدام AI اقراء هذا الخبر ياصديقي أداة Trivy لفحص الثغرات تعرضت لاختراق "Supply Chain" خطير، والنسخة الملغمة وتسببت في سرقت بيانات حساسة من المطورين والشركات.

🚨 قبل تنزل اداة فحص ثغرات من مطور مستقل او مبرمجه باستخدام AI اقراء هذا الخبر ياصديقي أداة Trivy لفحص الثغرات تعرضت لاختراق "Supply Chain" خطير، والنسخة الملغمة وتسببت في سرقت بيانات حساسة من المطورين والشركات. وش السالفة بالضبط؟ 📍 النسخة 0.69.4 كانت Backdoored وتحتوي على Infostealer 📍 المهاجمين (TeamPCP) سيطروا على GitHub Actions 📍 أي Workflow يستخدم النسخ المصابة ينفذ الكود الخبيث قبل ما يشغل الفحص نفسه وش اللي سرقوه؟ 🛡️ مفاتيح SSH (Private/Public Keys) 🛡️ بيانات سحابية: AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, Docker 🛡️ ملفات .env ومتغيرات البيئة 🛡️ كلمات مرور قواعد البيانات (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB) 🛡️ايضا Tokens خاصة بـ CI/CD و Jenkins و GitLab 🛡️ مفاتيح TLS و VPN configurations 🛡️ ايضا Webhooks لـ Slack و Discord 🛡️ محافظ العملات الرقمية طريقة التسريب 💻 البيانات تُجمع وتُشفّر في ملف tpcp.tar.gz 💻 يُرسل لسيرفر C2 باسم aquasecurtiy.org (لاحظ التلاعب في الاسم) 💻 لو فشل الإرسال، ينشئ repo عام باسم tpcp-docs في حساب الضحية ويرفع البيانات هناك المصيبة الكبرى إن Aqua Security اعترفوا إنهم ما احتووا الاختراق الأول بشكل كامل: "We rotated secrets and tokens, but the process wasn't atomic and attackers may have been privy to refreshed tokens." لاتثق بشكل اعمى في هذا الوقت ياصديقي

Write a LinkedIn post for sales about workflow. Angle: Sharing this on behalf of the awesome work Jake Bivens and a few others from our team (Andrew Aiken, Aron Schuhmann, Mariam Shodeinde, Kiril Ivlev, and William Rogers).

Sharing this on behalf of the awesome work Jake Bivens and a few others from our team (Andrew Aiken, Aron Schuhmann, Mariam Shodeinde, Kiril Ivlev, and William Rogers). When founders are handling the GTM/sales process on their own, multithreading can quickly become a bottleneck. It’s hard to manage a full deal like a full-time AE while also running a company. And when it comes to enterprise deals, multithreading is critical to improving close rates. When we work with founders, we know they’re limited on resources. So we look for ways to streamline parts of the GTM process that drive a ton of value but are incredibly time-intensive. This started with an idea Andrew Aiken had after speaking with one of our founders. He has “Founding AE” in his DNA, so when he realized the founder needed help with multithreading and moving deals to the next stage, he brought the idea to Jake Bivens and the team. Now we have an ABM workflow any founder can use, starting right after the first discovery call. As Andrew puts it... "Anything to reduce that feedback loop and extra workload is a huge win." In the past, startups needed a $250K+/year sales hire managing accounts to effectively multithread enterprise deals. Today, you can do that with agentic workflows paired with human experts, as we’ve built at QC Growth. Jake put together a quick video walking through the flow. Check it out and let us know what you think. https://lnkd.in/eXf559F2

Write a LinkedIn post for healthcare about workflow. Angle: Administrative costs are rising.

Administrative costs are rising. Staff shortages are worsening. Margins are under pressure. Incremental automation won’t fix this. Healthcare organisations need Agentic AI to change the operating model. This isn’t tools replacing tasks. It’s intelligent orchestration redesigning workflows. If you’re evaluating AI agents across clinical or operational workflows, this guide will help you move from isolated pilots to enterprise impact. P.S. Which workflow in your organization needs automation first?

Write a LinkedIn post for data about workflow. Angle: We are excited to share our latest peer-reviewed publication titled “AEC Co-design workflow for cross-domain querying and reasoning using Semantic Web Technologies”, now available in Automation in Construction (Elsevier, Volume 176, 2025): https://lnkd.in/eNc9kQny.

We are excited to share our latest peer-reviewed publication titled “AEC Co-design workflow for cross-domain querying and reasoning using Semantic Web Technologies”, now available in Automation in Construction (Elsevier, Volume 176, 2025): https://lnkd.in/eNc9kQny. This publication is the result of an interdisciplinary collaboration between the Institute for Computational Design and Construction (ICD), Department for Computing in Architecture (ICD/CA); the Institute for Artificial Intelligence (KI), both at the University of Stuttgart; and Buro Happold. The research was conducted as part of IntCDC Research Project 20 (RP20) within the Cluster of Excellence IntCDC | University of Stuttgart. The paper presents a co-design workflow that enables data from different disciplines to be integrated, queried, and reasoned over in a knowledge graph using Semantic Web Technologies such as the Web Ontology Language (OWL), the Resource Description Framework (RDF) data model, and AEC ontologies. This integration facilitates semantic interoperability and supports cross-disciplinary design decision-making, as demonstrated in a timber structure case study. A key feature of the workflow is a bidirectional mapping strategy that translates object-oriented data models—demonstrated with the Building Habitat Object Model (BHoM)—to and from RDF/OWL formats. A comparative evaluation against alternative approaches was conducted to assess the workflow’s effectiveness in supporting interdisciplinary collaboration and the detection of design constraints. Congratulations to the first author Diellza Elshani, and co-authors Alessio Lombardi, Daniel Hernández, Steffen Staab, Al Fisher and Thomas Wortmann. This work was supported by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) - German Research Foundation under Germany’s Excellence Strategy – EXC 2120/1 – 390831618 and COFFEE – STA 572_15-2. The open-access paper can be read through the following link: https://lnkd.in/eNc9kQny Institute for Computational Design and Construction (ICD), Department for Computing in Architecture Institute for Artificial Intelligence (KI) Cluster of Excellence IntCDC | University of Stuttgart University of Stuttgart Buro Happold © Video credits: Elshani et al. (2025), ICD/CA Universität Stuttgart © Soundtrack: Techno Racer by Sample Magic #icd #intcdc #exc2120 #unistuttgart #universityofstuttgart #aec #data #semantics #architecture #interoperability #computationaldesign #linkeddata #bim #graphs #knowledgegraphs #semanticweb #datavalidation #knowledgeengineering #ontologies #dataintegration #designscience #informationmodeling #computationalworkflows #semanticinteroperability #automationinconstruction #elsevier #publication #journal #bhom #dfg #openaccess #peerreviewed #rdf #owl #ai #sustainability #education #innovation #management #technology #digital #tech #semanticaI #academicresearch #semanticwebtechnologies #artificialintelligence #digitaltwins

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: Everyone tracks new logos.

Everyone tracks new logos. But is that the best measure of whether your product actually works and is loved? One of the metrics we obsess over at Jolt tells a truer story of product love and value: internal adoption velocity. How quickly does Jolt (our AI coding tool for production codebases) spread in an org? Does usage grow week after week? Does it expand to new teams? It's the real signal that people genuinely love the product, keep coming back, and tell their coworkers about it. It cuts through polite feedback - folks wouldn't keep dedicating more time to a product unless they're getting real value from it. We track this metric from day 1. One larger customer we've had grew their Jolt usage an average of 95% MoM over the last 6 months, and a newer, smaller team's usage is growing 258% WoW since starting 5 weeks ago. Obviously, this kind of growth isn't sustainable within a single account, but the trend gives us invaluable insight into genuine user adoption. It tells us engineers are not only trying Jolt but embedding it into their workflow and sharing it. #saas #metrics #AI #coding #devtools

Write a LinkedIn post for sales about workflow. Angle: Most people using AI at work are missing something important.

Most people using AI at work are missing something important. Not because the tools are bad. Because they’re using them the wrong way. In this episode of the Sell Better Daily Show, Jed Mahrle sat down with Kyle Vamvouris to unpack what sellers actually need to understand about AI right now. They didn’t talk about "the hype." They talked about how it actually works. One concept alone completely changes how you use tools like ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. And once you understand it, a lot of frustrating AI outputs suddenly make sense. If you’re using AI for things like: • prospecting • writing emails • analyzing calls • building sales materials This conversation will likely change how you approach it. Watch the replay here: https://lnkd.in/e2-4WNSy Curious what others are seeing too. What’s one task you’re using AI for right now that actually saves you time? #saywhatsales #sellbetter #ArtificialIntelligence #AI #SalesTech #B2BSales

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: I've been building ABM landing pages for target accounts using Claude Code.

I've been building ABM landing pages for target accounts using Claude Code. These are deeply researched ones. Pages that reference the account's specific pain, their tech stack, their business goals, and Docket's positioning mapped directly to their situation. Here's what the workflow actually looks like. I have a skill that tells Claude Code exactly how to research a target account... their exec's linkedin posts to find business goals, their website, their LinkedIn, their job postings, their tech stack signals. And a separate skill that has our full product-marketing context loaded: ICP, proof points, competitive angles, messaging by persona. Claude Code runs both before writing a single line of HTML. It doesn't start from a blank page. It starts from a brief that took it three minutes to build and would have taken me two hours. The page gets generated from a template, pushed to Vercel, and is live on a production URL in under fifteen minutes from the moment I type the account name. The output is legitimately good. Not "decent for AI." Good enough that I wouldn't be embarrassed sending it to a VP of Demand Gen at a 2,000-person company. The thing that makes it work is the context loading upfront. The skill files. The product-marketing doc. The structured research prompt. All of that exists before Claude writes the first word. Most people using AI for personalization are doing find-and-replace with a company name. I would argue that it's just mail merge with extra steps. The actual unlock is giving the model enough context that it can make editorial decisions. What to emphasize for this account. What pain to lead with. What proof point is most relevant. That only happens if you've done the work to load it up properly before you start.

Write a LinkedIn post for ai about workflow. Angle: Every B2B brand swears they want to stand out.

Every B2B brand swears they want to stand out. But then they Use the same blog header stock photo of a woman pointing at a screen The same webinar promo with a headshot, a title, and a gradient. The same $100k conference booth that's just a logo on a banner. Shit, I’ve done it too. When you're an understaffed marketing team shipping content every week, you don't have the time or budget to art direct every asset. So we grab an old template and move on. The result: everyone's content looks the same. Not because the ideas are the same — but because the constraints forced us into the same box. But that box is disappearing. The gap between creative vision and creative execution is collapsing. And that's a GOOD thing. It used to require jumping between tools and long feedback loops. But now agents keep the workflow aligned. That's what Luma AI is building — an agentic AI workspace where you direct the creative vision and AI agents orchestrate the execution. The differentiator is about to shift. It won't be who has the biggest design team or the most budget. It'll be who has the best taste and the sharpest point of view. Most of us got into marketing to bring ideas to life. But somewhere along the way, we got buried in template libraries and "can you make the logo bigger" requests. New AI tools give us a chance to get back to what matters — the thinking, the taste, the creative ambition. The "boring era" of B2B content is ending. The question is whether you're ready to be different now that you finally have the tools to be. Check out Luma → https://lnkd.in/g2aY2jkn #ai #partnerpost

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: AI is not a magic bullet.

AI is not a magic bullet. It’s a line item. Anthropic just did something most AI companies avoid saying out loud: they admitted that running large models at scale is still brutally expensive. Internally, they cut their 2025 gross margin forecast down to ~40% — not because demand dried up, but because cloud costs on Google Cloud and AWS came in about 23% higher than expected. To me, that’s the part a lot of AI narratives skip over: - Training is a one-time hit. Serving intelligence is the meter that never stops running. - These companies look less like pure software and more like infrastructure businesses tied to GPUs, energy, and hyperscaler pricing. Margins don’t magically appear just because you “add AI” — they show up when you have a clear system, use cases, and measurable savings or revenue on the other side. The same is true inside brands. If you bolt AI onto every workflow without a plan, you’re not “getting ahead,” you’re just adding cost and complexity. The teams that will actually see ROI are the ones who: - Design where AI should sit (and where it shouldn’t) - Define expected time savings and quality improvements up front - Instrument how they’ll measure that value before they start wiring in agents and copilots At Avenue Z, we try to keep both sides of the story in view: - The external narrative that treats AI as effortless, high-margin growth - And the operator reality where people are revising models, absorbing higher bills, and still choosing to invest for the long term — because they have a roadmap, not a collection of experiments We walked through what Anthropic’s margin revision actually means, and why we see it as a sign of maturity (not failure) — and a good reminder that AI needs strategy, not just budget: https://lnkd.in/ebeUiUH7 #AIeconomy #AIstrategy #Anthropic #CloudCosts #AIROI #EnterpriseAI

Write a LinkedIn post for data about workflow. Angle: After yesterday’s post on the roles that make healthcare AI actually work… a lot of people asked me the next question: “Okay—but who’s actually hiring for this?” Short answer?

After yesterday’s post on the roles that make healthcare AI actually work… a lot of people asked me the next question: “Okay—but who’s actually hiring for this?” Short answer? Almost everyone. But not always in the way you’d expect. Health systems are hiring clinical informaticists, data leaders, and integration roles—but they’re calling it “optimization,” “transformation,” or “digital strategy.” Big tech is hiring aggressively—but they’re looking for people who understand clinical workflows, not just models. Startups are moving fast—but the ones gaining traction all have one thing in common: they’re building around real care delivery, not just technology. And consulting firms? They’re quietly scaling teams to help health systems fix the exact gaps we’ve been talking about—workflow, data, and adoption. Here’s what I’m seeing clearly: The market isn’t just looking for “AI talent.” It’s looking for people who can connect the dots. Clinical + Data + Workflow + Technology Because that’s where most organizations are stuck. They don’t need another tool. They need someone who can walk in and say: → This is where the workflow breaks → This is where the data fails → This is why adoption isn’t happening And more importantly… how to fix it. That’s the real role that’s emerging. Not always labeled. Not always obvious. But absolutely in demand. If you’re in healthcare right now, this is one of the biggest opportunities I’ve seen in years. Curious—are you seeing organizations hiring for this directly… or still trying to fit it into existing roles? #HealthcareAI #DigitalHealth #ClinicalInformatics #HealthTech #HealthcareLeadership #AIinHealthcare #DigitalTransformation #HealthIT #ChangeManagement

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: 💬 “We’ve spent millions on cloud and AI.

💬 “We’ve spent millions on cloud and AI. Where’s the ROI?” 👂 I hear this constantly from clients. ✅ The technology works. ✅ The teams are capable. ⚠️ But many organizations still struggle to clearly connect cloud and AI investments to tangible, measurable business outcomes. Here’s the fundamental disconnect I keep seeing: 🤖 AI promises efficiencies at scale and the elimination of repetitive work, yet most organizations still approach AI as isolated use cases rather than enterprise software development. That’s the critical mistake. Scaling AI across workloads, business systems, and workflows requires treating it like any mission-critical software platform. ✍️ I wrote this blog to share what I’m seeing across clients today, and why cloud strategy, AI adoption, and financial governance need to be treated as core enterprise capabilities, not a collection of disconnected initiatives. We Used Claude Agent to Train an Open LLM 🤯 Not just to write the training script, but to actually submit jobs to cloud GPUs, monitor progress, and push finished models to the Hugging Face Hub. You just say something like: “Fine-tune Qwen3-0.6B on the dataset open-r1/codeforces-cots.” And it handled the rest. → Picks the right cloud GPUs → Validates the dataset → Runs the job (test or full) → Shares a live dashboard → Pushes checkpoints to HF No manual setup. No babysitting runs. This is what happens when agents have Skills. You don’t prompt workflows. You delegate outcomes. And we didn’t stop at model training. At TestFlow AI, agents now run marketing, sales, product, onboarding, even fine-tune the models itself. Agents aren’t assistants anymore. They’re operators. Full tutorial and article in the comments ⤵️

The real question for C-suites: Are you realizing ROI from your Epic investment, or just running a connected system? Key levers for moving from integration → intelligence: Structuring unstructured data (NLP, AI) Applying domain-specific intelligence to generate meaningful insights Embedding insights directly into clinical, operational, and revenue workflows Because in today’s healthcare landscape, having Epic isn’t enough. Making Epic work, intelligently is where competitive advantage lies. Write a LinkedIn post for cybersecurity about workflow. Angle: 🔐 Proud moment for the Splunk + Amazon Web Services (AWS) partnership.

🔐 Proud moment for the Splunk + Amazon Web Services (AWS) partnership. AWS Security Hub Extended launched today, and Splunk is one of a select group of curated partners included at launch. For AWS customers, this means Splunk's industry-leading SIEM and security operations capabilities are now available directly through the Security Hub console, with findings automatically aggregated alongside GuardDuty, Inspector, and other AWS-native signals. SOC analysts get correlated, context-rich data across their entire cloud and hybrid environment. The end result is that enterprise security teams can stop stitching tools together and start responding to what matters, faster, with more context, across their entire AWS estate. Incredibly proud of both teams. This is what a real strategic partnership looks like. Read the full announcement here: https://lnkd.in/gBX3jGBQ Also check out our executive Q&A here: https://lnkd.in/ggtYEP7F #AWS #Splunk #CloudSecurity #SecurityHub #SIEM #Cybersecurity #AWSPartner

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: There's a pattern I keep seeing with AI agents that bothers me.

There's a pattern I keep seeing with AI agents that bothers me. People build them to mirror their org chart. A finance agent. A marketing agent. A CTO agent. It feels like the obvious thing to do, which is usually a sign you're not thinking hard enough. I know because I did it too initially. Human orgs are shaped by a constraint: one person can only know so much. So we specialize. We create silos. Then we spend half our time coordinating across them. That's not a feature of good organization. It's the cost of being human. AI doesn't have that constraint. So why import it? I hit this recently while prepping a customer demo. I needed course design, systems engineering, and business development thinking; all in one workflow. With people, that's three calendars, two meetings, and a week of back-and-forth. With one agent, it was a single activity. Not because the agent was a generalist who knew a little about each. Because it was deeply competent in all three simultaneously. That's the thing most people miss. AI agents aren't generalists. They're multi-dimensional specialists. There's no human equivalent of that, which is exactly why copying human structures is the wrong starting point. Don't ask "which role should this agent fill?" Ask "which boundaries between roles shouldn't exist anymore?"
You write high-signal LinkedIn posts that are concrete, specific, and non-generic.
Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: Most CDP evaluations fail before the first demo because companies ask the wrong questions.

Most CDP evaluations fail before the first demo because companies ask the wrong questions. They ask: “Which CDP has the best features?” The better question is: “Which CDP fits how our company actually operates?” We see the same pattern over and over. A team decides they need a CDP to scale marketing. They start comparing: - composable vs packaged platforms - AI features - integrations - pricing tiers Three months later they are more confused than when they started. Why? Because CDPs don’t fail at the feature level. They fail at the implementation level. The companies that get this right do one thing differently. They start with use cases. Not feature lists. Questions like: - What workflows must be automated? - What systems must remain in the stack? - What data needs to move in real time? - What breaks if identity resolution fails? Once those answers are clear, most CDP options eliminate themselves. The right platform becomes obvious. We broke this entire process down in a new article. If you're evaluating a CDP this year, it will save you from expensive mistakes. See the link in the first comment. Curious what others ran into during their CDP selection. What surprised you the most?

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Last week I said the tempo was about to turn up, and all the support has been overwhelmingly motivating.

Last week I said the tempo was about to turn up, and all the support has been overwhelmingly motivating. I was going to wait a few more weeks to say what's next but ... I've never been the most patient person. So, I co-founded Neural Partners with two other legends of their craft. Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: I've spent at least 60' every day for the last couple of months vibe coding on Claude Code...

I've spent at least 60' every day for the last couple of months vibe coding on Claude Code... and most of the time I did it wrong! Defining the right anatomy of the .claude/ folder has defined a before and an after on the quality of my side projects. Most new users skip the setup. They open Claude Code and start prompting raw. No structure. No rules. No memory. That's the mistake I did. The .claude folder is Claude's operating system for your project. Get it right and Claude stops guessing. Get it wrong and you spend half your time correcting it. Here's the anatomy that works: 👉 CLAUDE.md → Claude's instruction manual. Build commands, architecture decisions, conventions, gotchas. Keep it under 200 lines. This is your highest-leverage file. 👉 rules/ → When CLAUDE.md gets crowded, split by concern. code-style.md, testing.md, api-conventions.md. Scope rules to specific paths with YAML frontmatter so they only load when relevant. 👉 commands/ → Repeatable workflows as slash commands. Code review, issue fixing, deploy checks. They run shell commands and inject real output into the prompt. 👉 skills/ → Like commands but Claude triggers them automatically when the task matches. They're packages, not single files. 👉 agents/ → Isolated subagent personas with their own tools and model preferences. A code reviewer that only reads. A security auditor scoped to grep. 👉 settings.json → Permission control. What Claude can run freely, what it must ask about, what's blocked entirely. The part most people miss: there are two .claude folders. One in your project committed, shared with the team and one at ~/.claude/ personal, global across all repos. The .claude folder is infrastructure. Treat it like one. Full guide in the article below in the comments 👇 #ai #claude

Write a LinkedIn post for fintech about workflow. Angle: "One-shot" from prompt to artifact It's so often vague what AI and agents can deliver.

"One-shot" from prompt to artifact It's so often vague what AI and agents can deliver. For us at Pipe, it's killing the ordinary workflows that quietly drain hours from risk and capital markets. I spent time in a 1:1 with an engineer this week, and something stuck with me. A concrete example: our borrowing base process. On bad days it looks like this: 1. Pull data from multiple systems (warehouse, bank, pooling accounts) 2. Export, re-shape, and VLOOKUP through BigQuery + CSVs 3. Copy/paste into a complex Excel template on a specific day 4. Check nothing breaks before a reporting deadline It's not "broken." It just burns time and attention in one of the highest-leverage parts of the business. Pipe engineer changed the flow: 1. Define the outcome as a business artifact, not a task list: a borrowing base report that is correct, on time, and reproducible. 2. Use AI and agents for the glue work: wiring APIs, shaping data, filling the right cells in the right template. 3. Rely on human to sign off: Would I sign my name on this borrowing request? The goal isn't "no humans in the loop." It's no humans tabbing between six tools and three spreadsheets just to kick off the work. Zoom out. A lot of our internal work fits this pattern: → Collections workflows jumping from CRM to dialer to notes to reporting → Risk reviews that start in a dashboard, move to SQL, then end in a slide → Partner performance pacing that lives in a BI tool, then gets re-built in a spreadsheet for a meeting My take: the real near-term impact of AI in fintech comes from driving up internal productivity. Stitching together domain-heavy workflows (contract covenants, geographic requirements, money movement) that completely define how fast and safely you can move. What one or two workflows have you made "one-shot" from prompt to artifact? (If you're like me, you're hearing Eminem's Lose Yourself when you read "one-shot") Vijay Vachani Jin Han Nate Wiger Logan Allin Marlon C. Nichols Albert Periu Nick Clemente

Write a LinkedIn post for ai about workflow. Angle: Nobody actually needs an AI agent to automate their business.

Nobody actually needs an AI agent to automate their business. There. I said it. I've been deep in the trenches building automation systems for founders, agencies, and ops teams. And the #1 mistake I see? They reach for AI before they've fixed the manual process. Here's the truth nobody in this space will tell you: A broken process, automated, is just a faster broken process. Before you build a single n8n workflow answer these 5 questions: 1. Can you describe the process step-by-step from memory? 2. Does a human currently do it consistently? 3. Do you know exactly where it fails? 4. Is the output measurable? 5. Would you trust a junior hire to do it with a checklist? If you answered No to 2 or more you don't have an automation problem. You have a process problem. Fix that first. Then automate. The best AI automation I've ever built saved 18 hours/week. It replaced a process that already worked perfectly manually. That's the secret. What's one process you've tried to automate too early? Drop it below, let's troubleshoot it together. 👇 #automation #n8n #aiworkflows #processdesign #nocode #operationsmanagement

Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: Yesterday, Latio named Formal as a Cloud Security Segment Leader Since then, I wrote a blog of some of my thoughts on the future of privileged access management and how Formal is primely positioned to usher in the next wave of PAM A few high level thoughts: 1.

Yesterday, Latio named Formal as a Cloud Security Segment Leader Since then, I wrote a blog of some of my thoughts on the future of privileged access management and how Formal is primely positioned to usher in the next wave of PAM A few high level thoughts: 1. Privileged Access Management has re-emerged as the critical enforcement plane. Everyone told me that Privileged Access Management (PAM) was an old, stagnant category not the future. Our customers and now this award tell a different story. 2 Access control should be as programmable and observable as your infrastructure itself. We should treat access control like any other developer workflow and what differentiates is DevX. 3. The new frontier of access isn’t human – it’s agentic. We need to extend PAM’s guardrails from human users to AI agents in real time because agents are increasingly getting access to more data. 4. Visibility and control belong in one platform. The industry has always put these into separate categories with separate tooling but that has caused fragmented enforcement of controls. Formal’s mission is to give security and engineering teams a single enforcement layer for everything that touches sensitive data – human, service, or AI. We understand that the boundaries of cloud security are expanding, but the principle remains the same: trust is earned through context, not static permissions. Full blog post in comments

Vibe marketing is becoming its own lane. And if you’re still doing marketing the 2021 way, you’re going to feel it… fast. Because the shift isn’t “use Canva better” or “edit quicker in CapCut.” The shift is AI-native creation where the bottleneck isn’t production, it’s taste, velocity, and distribution. The harsh reality: If your stack is still mostly traditional tools (Canva, Imgflip, Grammarly, CapCut, etc.), you’re competing like it’s manual labor… while everyone else is building factories. Yes, if you don't integrate any form of AI into your current workflow, you will be left behind. So here’s my current AI-era toolkit: My AI Marketing Stack (2026): ChatGPT / Claude: strategy, hooks, positioning, scripts, rewrites Nano Banana: image generation + rapid iteration Higgsfield / Kling /HeyGen: video generation (avatars, scenes, edits) Wispr Flow: voice-to-text (thinking out loud → content instantly) The real question: what am I missing for the next level? If you’re already running an AI-native stack, drop your must-have tools. I want to have the modern marketer’s toolkit before the gap becomes uncatchable.

Write a LinkedIn post for cybersecurity about workflow. Angle: 🛡️ Zero-Trust Architecture in Action: An Integrated Security System for Automated Threat Detection and Response As a student pursuing my Bachelor of Technology in Network Technology at the University of Vocational Technology, Sri Lanka, I recently developed a comprehensive cybersecurity project with my team as my final year project.

🛡️ Zero-Trust Architecture in Action: An Integrated Security System for Automated Threat Detection and Response As a student pursuing my Bachelor of Technology in Network Technology at the University of Vocational Technology, Sri Lanka, I recently developed a comprehensive cybersecurity project with my team as my final year project. In today’s threat landscape traditional passive firewalls are no longer enough, we must assume networks will be breached. Powered by a Wazuh SIEM and a pfSense border gateway, our Zero Trust architecture doesn't just log attacks ,it actively deceives adversaries and autonomously isolates them in milliseconds. Here is a deep dive into the system's three core defense mechanisms: 🪤 Proactive Deception (The Cowrie Honeypot) Instead of outright dropping external SSH scans, internal iptables silently redirect suspicious traffic to a Cowrie honeypot mimicking a vulnerable Linux system. The Mechanics: When an attacker launches a brute force attack, the honeypot intentionally accepts weak credentials. The Response: As the adversary wastes time, Cowrie generates JSON telemetry. The SIEM extracts their IP, immediately updating a global threat feed (blocklist.txt). pfSense ingests this, instantly dropping all Layer 3/4 traffic from the adversary enterprise-wide. 🌐 Application Layer Defense (ModSecurity WAF) To protect critical web infrastructure, we deployed a strict Layer 7 defense in depth strategy. The Mechanics: ModSecurity operates as a reverse proxy utilizing the OWASP Core Rule Set for deep packet inspection. The Response: If an attacker attempts an SQL Injection (?id=1' OR '1'='1), the WAF terminates the connection (403 Forbidden). The Wazuh Agent forwards this anomaly to the SIEM, which dynamically reconfigures the perimeter firewall to sever all network communication with the hostile IP. 💻 Host Based Containment (Windows Server EDR) To combat "Insider Threats" that have breached the perimeter, our Windows Servers are equipped with Wazuh EDR agents. The Mechanics: If a compromised internal machine attempts lateral movement via SMB brute forcing, local Security Event Logs immediately register the failures. The Response: The SIEM detects this and fires a localized Active Response. Using native netsh commands, it autonomously writes a new Windows Firewall rule that surgically isolates the server from the compromised internal IP, stopping the spread without human intervention. Building this from the ground up from Linux dependencies to custom Active Response scripts was an incredible journey into SOC operations and incident response. | ✍️ | © Kasun Sathsara #Cybersecurity #ZeroTrust #Wazuh #SIEM #pfSense #BlueTeam #NetworkSecurity #ModSecurity #ThreatIntelligence #IncidentResponse #UniversityOfVocationalTechnology #InfoSec

Write a LinkedIn post for healthcare about workflow. Angle: Prior authorization is back in the spotlight and for good reason: 94% of physicians report care delays due to prior auth, and 18% say it has led to a serious adverse event.

Prior authorization is back in the spotlight and for good reason: 94% of physicians report care delays due to prior auth, and 18% say it has led to a serious adverse event. But what often gets missed is that most prior auth breakdowns aren't clinical, they're administrative. The friction usually has less to do with medical necessity and more to do with missing documentation, inconsistent benefit information, manual portal entry, fax back-and-forth, and endless phone calls. Payers operate at machine speed. Many providers are still navigating PDFs and phone trees. That imbalance is what patients ultimately feel. CMS's Interoperability and Prior Authorization Rule changes that dynamic. For providers, this isn't just another compliance update but a forcing function to modernize. Regulated AI and workflow automation are becoming core infrastructure for timely access to care. I wrote more about what this rule means and what healthcare organizations should be doing now in my op-ed with MedCity News Read my piece here: https://lnkd.in/eqzuXwwq

Write a LinkedIn post for healthcare about workflow. Angle: Your healthcare organization already has the data foundation, so why do so many AI projects start by demanding a massive migration?

Your healthcare organization already has the data foundation, so why do so many AI projects start by demanding a massive migration? From HIMSS, we sit down with Michael Campbell, Chief Product Officer Hyland to talk about a more realistic path: use what you already have, especially the unstructured content that dominates healthcare operations, and apply agentic AI where it can actually remove work. We get specific about healthcare data silos and fragmentation across EHR ecosystems, enterprise content platforms, and tools like SharePoint. Hyland’s approach leans into data federation, keeping content where it is while still unlocking automation, insight, and faster workflows. We also dig into the real-world use cases that matter when margins are thin: revenue cycle efficiency, intelligent medical records, and intelligent correspondence that reduces administrative load and helps teams do more without adding headcount. Then we pressure-test the hype. “Just feed it into an LLM” breaks down quickly in healthcare, where governance, security, structure, and regulatory compliance are non-negotiable. We talk through what it takes to balance speed with safety, and why being LLM agnostic can reduce vendor lock-in as models change week to week. If you’re exploring healthcare AI, workflow automation, and responsible use of generative AI, you’ll leave with a clearer model for getting quick wins and building a value roadmap that sticks. #HIMSS26 Irma Rastegayeva✨Influence Through Storytelling™️ eViRa Health #AI #Healthcare Hyland Healthcare

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: For the past few weeks, I’ve been building a project around a frustration I kept facing with AI coding tools.

For the past few weeks, I’ve been building a project around a frustration I kept facing with AI coding tools. Most AI agents can read files. But very few can actually work with a codebase as a living system. Context gets stale. Important relationships get missed. And the bigger the repo gets, the worse it gets. So I started building NexusCode. A codebase intelligence server that continuously indexes repositories, stays fresh with GitHub webhooks, and exposes real codebase context through MCP tools and APIs so AI agents can: • Search codebases • Ask contextual questions • Plan implementations • Automate engineering workflows The goal is simple: Move from “AI that reads files” to “AI that understands codebases.” Because the next generation of developer tools will be won by context quality, not just model quality. Still early, but excited about where this is going. 🔗 https://lnkd.in/d6pgkg6c #AI #AIAgents #DeveloperTools #DevTools #OpenSource #SoftwareEngineering #LLM #BuildInPublic #EngineeringProductivity #MCP

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: Recently, I built something practical — a customer support workflow for a small craft and training business, Awushi Green Center.

Recently, I built something practical — a customer support workflow for a small craft and training business, Awushi Green Center. The goal was simple: Make it easy for customers to send enquiries and book training sessions without messages getting lost. While testing a helpdesk setup using @fr Freshdesk, I ran into a limitation on the plan I'm using. Website visitors couldn’t submit support requests directly through the default form. At first, it looked like a blocker. But instead of stopping there, I asked: How can we still make this work? So I designed a workaround. I connected a support form to an automation workflow using Zapier so that every submission automatically generated a support ticket in the helpdesk system. At the same time: • Web chat was enabled for real-time enquiries • Agents were onboarded into a shared workspace using Slack • A booking system was embedded using Calendly so customers could schedule training sessions The result was a simple workflow: Customer enquiry → automation trigger → ticket creation → agent notification → resolution. This small experiment reminded me that technology isn’t just about tools. It’s about designing systems that help businesses manage work and respond to customers more efficiently. I’ll share the workflow diagram in my next post. Have you ever had to design a workaround because a tool limitation blocked your workflow? #CustomerSupport #Automation #CRMOps #WorkflowDesign
